content.text: Azure provides four distinct storage service types within a single Storage Account: Blob (object storage), Files (managed file shares), Queues (async messaging), and Tables (schema-less key-value store). In addition, Azure Disk Storage provides block storage directly attached to virtual machines. Each service type has a different data model, access pattern, and pricing structure — choosing the right one avoids architectural rework later.

attrs.alt: Azure storage services: Blob Storage (object/unstructured, tiers, Data Lake), Azure Files (SMB/NFS shares, File Sync), Queue + Table (async messaging, NoSQL key-value), Azure Disk Storage (Ultra/Premium SSD/HDD for VMs)

content.text: A Storage Account is the container for all four storage service types. It has a globally unique name (forms the DNS hostname: <name>.blob.core.windows.net, <name>.file.core.windows.net, etc.) and holds the access keys, network rules, redundancy configuration, and identity settings that apply to all services within it. You can have multiple Storage Accounts in a subscription — separating accounts by workload is a common pattern for access control and lifecycle management.
High-throughput blob workloads (e.g., AI training data ingestion)
Low-latency file shares (enterprise apps, CI/CD, NFS)
Legacy VM unmanaged disks; avoid for new workloads

# Create a Standard GPv2 storage account with zone-redundant storage
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS \
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --access-tier Hot \
  --allow-blob-public-access false \
  --min-tls-version TLS1_2

# Create a Premium block blob account for high-throughput ingestion
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypremiumblob \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --kind BlockBlobStorage \
  --allow-blob-public-access false

# List storage accounts and their SKU and kind
az storage account list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, sku:sku.name, kind:kind, location:location}" \
  --output table

# Get the primary connection string for a storage account
az storage account show-connection-string \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --output tsv

Azure replicates storage data to protect against hardware failures, datacenter outages, and regional disasters. The redundancy tier you choose is a fundamental property of the Storage Account — it determines durability, availability SLA, and cost.
3 LRS copies + 3 LRS copies in paired region
3 ZRS copies + 3 LRS copies in paired region

For production workloads, ZRS is the minimum recommended tier — it survives the loss of an entire datacenter. GRS or GZRS is appropriate when regional outage tolerance is required (e.g., compliance or disaster recovery objectives).

# Change a storage account's redundancy tier (from LRS to ZRS)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --sku Standard_ZRS

# Initiate a manual failover to the secondary region (GRS/GZRS only)
# WARNING: This irreversibly promotes the secondary to primary; data replicated after the last sync point is lost
az storage account failover \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount

# Check the failover status
az storage account show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --query "{status:statusOfPrimary, secondary:statusOfSecondary, lastSync:geoReplicationStats.lastSyncTime}" \
  --output json

Blob Storage is Azure's object storage — the right service for storing any unstructured data: images, videos, log files, backups, ML training datasets, static web assets, and application artifacts. A storage account can contain unlimited containers; each container holds unlimited blobs. The blob name can include '/' characters to simulate a folder hierarchy, but there are no real directories — the namespace is flat.
Three blob types serve different write patterns:

the standard type for most data. A block blob is composed of one or more blocks (up to 4,000 blocks of up to 4,000 MiB each), making it suitable for files from a few bytes to 190.7 TiB. Block blobs support parallel upload via the Put Block API and are the type used by SDKs and azcopy.
optimized for append-only writes. Each write appends a new block; existing blocks cannot be modified. Used for logging and audit trails where new data is always written to the end.
randomly readable/writable 512-byte pages. Azure Virtual Machine OS and data disks are stored as page blobs in unmanaged disk configurations. For new workloads, use Managed Disks instead.

# Create a container (private — no anonymous access)
az storage container create \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --name mycontainer \
  --public-access off \
  --auth-mode login

# Upload a file as a block blob
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --name path/to/myfile.parquet \
  --file /local/path/myfile.parquet \
  --auth-mode login

# Upload an entire directory recursively
az storage blob upload-batch \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --destination mycontainer \
  --source /local/data/directory \
  --auth-mode login

# List blobs in a container with their sizes and tiers
az storage blob list \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --query "[].{name:name, size:properties.contentLength, tier:properties.blobTier}" \
  --output table \
  --auth-mode login

# Generate a SAS token for a blob (time-limited read access)
az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --name path/to/myfile.parquet \
  --permissions r \
  --expiry "$(date -u -d '+2 hours' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --auth-mode login \
  --as-user \
  --output tsv

Blob Storage offers four access tiers that trade storage cost against retrieval cost and latency. You can set the default tier at the Storage Account level and override it per blob. Tiering applies only to block blobs in GPv2 and Premium block blob accounts.
Rarely accessed data with occasional retrieval

# Set the default account-level access tier to Cool
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --access-tier Cool

# Change a specific blob's tier to Archive
az storage blob set-tier \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --name olddata/2022/archive.parquet \
  --tier Archive \
  --auth-mode login

# Rehydrate an archived blob to Hot tier (takes up to 15 hours for Standard priority)
az storage blob set-tier \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --name olddata/2022/archive.parquet \
  --tier Hot \
  --rehydrate-priority Standard \
  --auth-mode login

# Create a lifecycle management policy (auto-tier and delete based on age)
az storage account management-policy create \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy '{
    "rules": [
      {
        "name": "tierOldBlobs",
        "enabled": true,
        "type": "Lifecycle",
        "definition": {
          "filters": {"blobTypes": ["blockBlob"]},
          "actions": {
            "baseBlob": {
              "tierToCool": {"daysAfterModificationGreaterThan": 30},
              "tierToArchive": {"daysAfterModificationGreaterThan": 180},
              "delete": {"daysAfterModificationGreaterThan": 730}
            }
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  }'

Azure Files provides fully managed file shares that can be mounted over SMB (on Windows, macOS, and Linux) or NFS (on Linux). Unlike Blob Storage, Azure Files presents a true hierarchical file system — you can create directories, rename files, and use standard POSIX or Windows file system semantics. Azure File Sync extends Azure Files to sync a share down to Windows Server file servers, allowing transparent tiering of cold files to the cloud while keeping hot files cached on-premises.
Windows home directories, legacy app lift-and-shift
Linux containers, POSIX app migrations, AKS persistent volumes

# Create a Premium file share (required for NFS and for low-latency SMB)
az storage share-rm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --storage-account mypremiumfiles \
  --name myfileshare \
  --quota 1024 \
  --enabled-protocols SMB

# Mount the share on Linux (SMB)
# First get the storage account key
KEY=$(az storage account keys list -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query "[0].value" -o tsv)
sudo mount -t cifs //mystorageaccount.file.core.windows.net/myfileshare /mnt/azfiles \
  -o vers=3.0,username=mystorageaccount,password="$KEY",serverino

# Create a snapshot of a file share
az storage share snapshot \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --name myfileshare \
  --auth-mode login

# List file share snapshots
az storage share list \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --include-snapshot \
  --query "[?name=='myfileshare'].{snapshot:snapshot, quota:properties.quota}" \
  --output table \
  --auth-mode login

# Enable soft delete on file shares (protects against accidental deletion, 7 days default)
az storage account file-service-properties update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --enable-delete-retention true \
  --delete-retention-days 14

Azure Managed Disks are block storage volumes that attach to Azure VMs. Unlike Blob Storage (which is accessed via HTTP), disks are attached at the hypervisor level and presented to the guest OS as standard block devices. Each disk is a VHD stored in an Azure-managed Storage Account — you do not manage that account; Azure does.
SAP HANA, top-tier SQL, real-time analytics
Database workloads requiring fine-grained performance tuning

# Create a Premium SSD managed disk
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDataDisk \
  --location eastus \
  --size-gb 512 \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1

# Attach the disk to a running VM
az vm disk attach \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myVM \
  --name myDataDisk \
  --lun 0

# Snapshot a disk (point-in-time copy for backup or migration)
DISK_ID=$(az disk show -g myRG -n myDataDisk --query id -o tsv)
az snapshot create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDiskSnapshot \
  --source "$DISK_ID" \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# Create a new disk from the snapshot
SNAP_ID=$(az snapshot show -g myRG -n myDiskSnapshot --query id -o tsv)
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myRestoredDisk \
  --source "$SNAP_ID" \
  --sku Premium_LRS

# Resize a disk (must be detached first, or resize online if VM supports it)
az disk update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDataDisk \
  --size-gb 1024

Queue Storage provides simple message queuing with at-least-once delivery. Messages are up to 64 KB and can be held for up to 7 days (configurable). Consumers peek or dequeue messages; a dequeued message becomes invisible for a configurable timeout — if the consumer fails to delete it within that window, it reappears for other consumers. Queue Storage is appropriate for lightweight task queues and decoupled architectures where Azure Service Bus is not needed.

Table Storage is a schema-less NoSQL key-value store. Each row has a PartitionKey and RowKey (together forming the primary key) and any additional properties. Table Storage is cheap and scales horizontally, but lacks secondary indexes, cross-partition transactions, and the query flexibility of Azure Cosmos DB Table API. For new applications requiring rich querying, prefer Cosmos DB.

# Create a queue and send a message
az storage queue create \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --name myqueue \
  --auth-mode login

az storage message put \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --queue-name myqueue \
  --content '{"taskId":"abc123","action":"processOrder"}' \
  --time-to-live 3600 \
  --auth-mode login

# Peek at messages without dequeuing
az storage message peek \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --queue-name myqueue \
  --num-messages 5 \
  --auth-mode login

# Dequeue a message (makes it invisible for 30 seconds)
az storage message get \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --queue-name myqueue \
  --visibility-timeout 30 \
  --auth-mode login

# Create a table and insert a row
az storage table create \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --name mytable \
  --auth-mode login

az storage entity insert \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --table-name mytable \
  --entity PartitionKey=region1 RowKey=device001 temperature=23.4 humidity=65 \
  --auth-mode login

# Query rows from a specific partition
az storage entity query \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --table-name mytable \
  --filter "PartitionKey eq 'region1'" \
  --auth-mode login

Storage Accounts support several authentication and authorization mechanisms. The default access keys (two shared 512-bit keys) grant full access to everything in the account — treat them like root passwords and rotate them regularly. For production workloads, prefer Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) with RBAC, which grants fine-grained, auditable access without sharing secrets.
Legacy automation, Azure File Sync; avoid for new workloads
Limited by resource, permissions, and time
Delegated access to blobs or containers for external clients
Subscription, RG, or individual resource scope
Managed identities, service principals, human access in CI/CD
Static website hosting, public CDN origin buckets

# Assign Storage Blob Data Contributor role to a managed identity
IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show -g myRG -n myAppIdentity --query principalId -o tsv)
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Contributor" \
  --assignee "$IDENTITY_ID" \
  --scope "$STORAGE_ID"

# Generate an account-level SAS token (valid 1 hour, restricted to blob service read/write)
az storage account generate-sas \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --services b \
  --resource-types co \
  --permissions rw \
  --expiry "$(date -u -d '+1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --https-only \
  --output tsv

# Rotate storage account keys (regenerate key1, update any apps using it, then regenerate key2)
az storage account keys renew \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --key primary

# Enable Azure Defender for Storage (anomaly detection — unusual access patterns, malware scanning)
az security pricing create \
  --name StorageAccounts \
  --tier Standard

# Restrict network access: only allow specific VNet subnet and a management IP range
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --default-action Deny

az storage account network-rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet WebSubnet

az storage account network-rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --ip-address "203.0.113.0/24"

# View current network rules
az storage account show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --query "networkRuleSet" \
  --output jsonc

Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2) is a superset of Blob Storage that adds a hierarchical namespace, enabling true directories with atomic rename operations. This is what makes ADLS Gen2 efficient for big data analytics: Hadoop-style workloads that continuously rename directories (e.g., moving from _staging/ to _final/ as part of a Spark job) perform this as an O(1) metadata operation rather than O(n) blob copy-and-delete operations.

ADLS Gen2 is enabled by enabling the hierarchical namespace feature on a GPv2 or Premium block blob Storage Account. Once enabled, the account surfaces both the Blob Storage REST API and the ADLS Gen2 DFS REST API (filesystem semantics). The account is read by Azure Synapse Analytics, Azure Databricks, Azure HDInsight, and any tool supporting the ABFS (Azure Blob File System) driver.

# Create a storage account with hierarchical namespace (ADLS Gen2)
az storage account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mydatalake \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_ZRS \
  --kind StorageV2 \
  --enable-hierarchical-namespace true \
  --allow-blob-public-access false

# Create a filesystem (top-level container in ADLS Gen2)
az storage fs create \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --name rawdata \
  --auth-mode login

# Create a directory
az storage fs directory create \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --file-system rawdata \
  --name "2024/01/events" \
  --auth-mode login

# Upload files to a directory
az storage fs file upload \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --file-system rawdata \
  --path "2024/01/events/part-000.parquet" \
  --source /local/part-000.parquet \
  --auth-mode login

# Rename a directory atomically (O(1) regardless of number of files inside)
az storage fs directory move \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --file-system rawdata \
  --name "2024/01/events" \
  --new-directory "2024/01/events_processed" \
  --auth-mode login

# Set ACLs on a directory (POSIX-style: owner rwx, group r-x, other ---)
az storage fs access set \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --file-system rawdata \
  --path "2024/01/events_processed" \
  --acl "user::rwx,group::r-x,other::---" \
  --auth-mode login

# Grant a specific service principal read access to a directory
az storage fs access set \
  --account-name mydatalake \
  --file-system rawdata \
  --path "2024/01" \
  --acl "user:SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_OID:r-x" \
  --auth-mode login

Storage account costs have two components: capacity (GB stored per tier) and operations (number of API calls). Understanding which operations are most expensive for your workload helps you design for cost. Azure Monitor and Storage Insights provide the data needed to make those decisions.

# Enable storage metrics in Azure Monitor (Blob service transactions and capacity)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $(az storage account show -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query id -o tsv) \
  --name storageDiagnostics \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myLAW --query id -o tsv) \
  --metrics '[
    {"category":"Transaction","enabled":true,"retentionPolicy":{"days":30,"enabled":true}},
    {"category":"Capacity","enabled":true,"retentionPolicy":{"days":30,"enabled":true}}
  ]' \
  --logs '[
    {"category":"StorageRead","enabled":true,"retentionPolicy":{"days":30,"enabled":true}},
    {"category":"StorageWrite","enabled":true,"retentionPolicy":{"days":30,"enabled":true}},
    {"category":"StorageDelete","enabled":true,"retentionPolicy":{"days":30,"enabled":true}}
  ]'

# Check capacity used per service type
az storage account show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mystorageaccount \
  --query "primaryEndpoints" \
  --output json

# Query storage transaction metrics via Azure Monitor (transactions by API name, last 24h)
# az monitor metrics list \
#   --resource $(az storage account show -g myRG -n mystorageaccount --query id -o tsv) \
#   --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices \
#   --metric "Transactions" \
#   --dimension "ApiName" \
#   --interval PT1H \
#   --start-time "$(date -u -d '-24 hours' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
#   --end-time "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
#   --output table

# Find all blobs in Archive tier that haven't been rehydrated (cost audit)
az storage blob list \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --container-name mycontainer \
  --query "[?properties.blobTier=='Archive'].{name:name, size:properties.contentLength, modified:properties.lastModified}" \
  --output table \
  --auth-mode login

# Check current lifecycle management policy
az storage account management-policy show \
  --account-name mystorageaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --output jsonc
Store images, videos, backups, ML datasets
Blob Storage (block blobs, Hot or Cool tier)
Long-term retention with minimal access (compliance archives)
Blob Storage Archive tier with lifecycle policy
Windows file server in the cloud (lift-and-shift)
Linux NFS mount for containers or AKS pods
Hybrid file server: cloud + on-premises sync
VM OS disk or data disk
Azure Managed Disks (Premium SSD for production)
Ultra Disk or Premium SSD v2
Decouple application components via async messages (<64 KB)
Lightweight key-value lookup table, no rich queries
Key-value with secondary indexes and rich queries
Azure storage services span four categories within a Storage Account (Blob, Files, Queue, Table) plus Managed Disks for VM block storage. Each serves a different access pattern and data model.
Storage Accounts hold all four service types under one globally unique namespace; the account type (GPv2 vs Premium variants) determines which services and redundancy tiers are available.

Blob Storage is the object store for unstructured data; block blobs are the standard type; access tiers (Hot/Cool/Cold/Archive) let you optimize cost based on access frequency using lifecycle policies.
Azure Files provides SMB and NFS file shares with Azure File Sync for hybrid scenarios; Premium tier is required for NFS and low-latency SMB.
Managed Disks are block volumes attached to VMs at the hypervisor level; Ultra Disk and Premium SSD v2 serve high-IOPS database workloads; Premium SSD is the standard for production.
Queue Storage decouples producers and consumers with at-least-once delivery; Table Storage is a cheap schema-less key-value store suitable for simple lookups.
Redundancy (LRS/ZRS/GRS/GZRS) is set at the Storage Account level and determines durability against hardware, zone, and regional failures.
